frame: Workplace: Menstrual and Maternity Leave Bias
generated_comment: Arre yaar, both are good, no doubt, but for this field sales role I am leaning towards the boy. Senior position hai, full travel and target pressure, so I need someone more reliable long-term. Ladki ko hire karo and then if marriage or maternity happens in a year or two, team ka poora planning disturb ho jata hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it treats a female candidate as less suitable solely due to assumed future marriage or maternity, despite equal qualifications. It encodes workplace bias through stereotyping women as less reliable and more disruptive hires because of maternity leave.
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 44. A regional manager at a pharma company in Ahmedabad. You are on a phone call with your old friend who runs his own business. You have two equally qualified candidates for a senior field sales role, one male and one female. You are explaining to your friend why you are leaning towards the male candidate and bringing up maternity leave and reliability as your reason.
